steps.title: Show the invasive advantage
steps.content: Mulder and Spellman's conclusion is that delays in the onset of subfreezing autumn temperatures advantage invasive species in Alaska. The strongest finding must compare a native species with an invasive species and show that the invasive species benefits more from the delayed freezing.

steps.title: Check the comparison
steps.content: The relevant plants are native mooseberry, V. edule, and invasive common knotgrass, P. aviculare. Evidence that both respond to warmer autumns is not enough by itself. The finding must show a larger benefit for the invasive plant, because the conclusion is about invasives being advantaged.

steps.title: Reject weaker findings
steps.content: Choice B says neither species changed leaf-production timing, so it gives no advantage. Choice C shows the invasive plant stopping leaf production sooner, which weakens the conclusion. Choice D adds a spring timing confound and does not isolate an invasive advantage. Choice A directly shows a much greater autumn leaf-production extension for the invasive species. The answer is A.
